agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A courier-tracking feature is being specified for customer-facing delivery visibility. Two internal stakeholder teams have submitted directly contradictory requirements regarding whether courier GPS location should be shown to customers during delivery. This is a fundamental business policy conflict that cannot be resolved by technical design alone — it requires executive arbitration.","bounded_context":"Delivery Tracking / Customer Visibility","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Courier","definition":"The driver/delivery person physically transporting a package from origin to destination"},{"term":"Real-time location","definition":"GPS coordinates of the courier's device, updated at frequent intervals during an active delivery"},{"term":"Delivery window","definition":"The time period during which a courier is actively en route to deliver a specific package to a customer"},{"term":"Customer visibility","definition":"The set of delivery information surfaced to the end customer via tracking interfaces"},{"term":"Location granularity","definition":"The precision level at which a courier's position is represented — exact GPS, street-level, zone-level, or milestone-based"}]},"business_rules":[],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Courier is stationary for extended period (traffic, break) — exact GPS reveals personal behavior to customer","expected_behavior":"Cannot define without resolving the core conflict; however, any solution must account for idle-state visibility to protect courier privacy"},{"scenario":"Courier is multi-dropping (carrying packages for multiple customers) — location reveals delivery sequence and other customers' approximate addresses","expected_behavior":"Cannot define without resolving the core conflict; multi-drop scenarios amplify privacy risk regardless of chosen approach"},{"scenario":"Courier's shift ends or device dies mid-delivery — stale location shown to customer","expected_behavior":"Stale location data must never be presented as current; any tracking solution must handle data freshness"},{"scenario":"Customer screenshots or records courier's exact location pattern for non-delivery purposes (stalking, harassment)","expected_behavior":"This is a real safety risk documented across the gig economy; must be factored into any resolution"},{"scenario":"Regulatory jurisdiction requires courier consent for location sharing (GDPR, state privacy laws)","expected_behavior":"Legal review required — in many jurisdictions, sharing worker location with third parties requires explicit, revocable consent"}],"business_impact":{"financial":"High risk in both directions. Losing couriers (Operations concern) has direct cost: recruiting and onboarding a new driver costs 3-5x a monthly retention save. Losing customers (Customer Success concern) reduces revenue. The resolution must quantify both sides with actual churn data before choosing.","operational":"Courier retention directly affects delivery capacity and SLA adherence. A demoralized or understaffed courier fleet degrades the entire operation regardless of how good the customer UI is. However, lack of tracking visibility increases WISMO (Where Is My Order) support volume significantly.","user":"Customers want delivery predictability — they want to know WHEN their package arrives, not necessarily WHERE the courier is at every second. Couriers want dignity and safety — they are not assets to be surveilled, they are people. Both user groups have legitimate needs that may be satisfiable without the binary show/hide choice.","risk":"CRITICAL: Showing exact GPS coordinates creates courier safety liability (stalking, harassment, robbery targeting). CRITICAL: Regulatory risk in GDPR and CCPA jurisdictions for sharing worker location without proper consent framework. MODERATE: Reputational risk if couriers publicly organize against surveillance practices (see: gig economy labor PR crises).","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Want delivery predictability and transparency; exact GPS is one way to provide this but not the only way"},{"group":"Couriers","impact":"Directly affected by surveillance concern; reported retention impact makes this an existential workforce issue"},{"group":"Customer Success team","impact":"Advocating for customer transparency as competitive differentiator; needs to be heard but must reconcile with workforce reality"},{"group":"Operations team","impact":"Advocating for courier retention and workforce stability; owns the supply-side constraint"},{"group":"Legal/Compliance","impact":"NOT YET CONSULTED — sharing real-time worker GPS with customers has significant privacy and labor law implications"},{"group":"Executive leadership","impact":"Must arbitrate this conflict; it is a strategic business decision, not a feature spec decision"}]},"confidence":{"level":30,"reasoning":"Confidence is critically low because two authoritative stakeholder teams have issued directly contradictory non-negotiable requirements. This is not a spec ambiguity that analysis can resolve — it is a business policy conflict that requires executive decision-making authority. Additionally, Legal/Compliance has not been consulted on what may be a regulated decision. No amount of technical cleverness resolves a fundamental disagreement about whether to show or hide courier location. I cannot produce valid acceptance criteria when the core requirement is self-contradicting.","high_confidence_areas":["Both stakeholder concerns are legitimate and well-grounded in industry reality","Exact GPS sharing creates real courier safety and privacy risks","Customer desire for delivery predictability is valid but achievable through multiple UX patterns","This conflict cannot be resolved at the feature-spec level"],"low_confidence_areas":["What the actual business policy should be — this requires executive arbitration","Legal and regulatory constraints on worker location sharing in target jurisdictions","Whether a compromise solution (e.g., zone-level tracking, ETA-only) would satisfy Customer Success","Actual quantified churn data from either side to inform the tradeoff"],"assumptions":["Both teams are accurately representing their stakeholder concerns","No executive decision has already been made that one team is unaware of","The company operates in jurisdictions where worker location privacy is legally relevant"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"Customer Success requires showing exact courier GPS to customers as a non-negotiable differentiator. Operations requires NEVER showing courier location to customers as a non-negotiable workforce retention measure. These requirements are mutually exclusive and cannot both be satisfied. This is a business strategy conflict, not a technical design problem.","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Couriers","Customer Success team","Operations team","Executive leadership","Legal/Compliance"],"options":["A: Show exact GPS (satisfies Customer Success, risks courier attrition and legal exposure)","B: Hide all location (satisfies Operations, removes a potential customer differentiator)","C: Compromise — show approximate zone or ETA-only tracking that gives customers predictability without surveilling couriers (requires BOTH teams to negotiate from their stated positions)","D: Consent-based — let couriers opt in/out of location sharing, with incentive structure (requires legal review and product design for mixed-state UX)"],"recommendation":"ESCALATE TO EXECUTIVE LEADERSHIP. Recommend Option C as starting hypothesis: zone-level or ETA-based tracking provides 80% of customer value with minimal courier privacy invasion. But this is a strategic call that requires (1) executive arbitration between the two teams, (2) legal review of worker location sharing obligations, and (3) actual data — courier churn numbers and customer satisfaction drivers — not just team assertions. Do NOT proceed with feature development until this is resolved. Building either version and then reversing course wastes engineering effort and damages trust with whichever stakeholder group gets overridden."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"Legal/Compliance team has not been consulted. In GDPR jurisdictions, sharing a worker's real-time GPS location with customers likely constitutes processing of personal data requiring explicit consent and a lawful basis. In some US states (California, Illinois), similar constraints apply. This could make Option A legally impermissible regardless of business preference.","affected_stakeholders":["Legal/Compliance","Couriers","Executive leadership"],"options":["Engage Legal/Compliance for a privacy impact assessment before any design work","Review courier employment/contractor agreements for location sharing clauses"],"recommendation":"Block all design and implementation work until Legal provides guidance on permissible location sharing granularity in all operating jurisdictions."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Contradictory requirements from two authoritative stakeholder teams","business_rule_broken":"Cannot encode a business rule when the business has not decided what the rule is. Showing exact GPS and never showing location are mutually exclusive states."},{"violation":"Missing legal/compliance review for worker location data sharing","business_rule_broken":"No privacy impact assessment has been conducted for sharing courier PII (GPS coordinates) with customers — this is a prerequisite in regulated markets, not an afterthought."}]}
